speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. Interview with Judith Velencei. We were talking about your schooling. Can we continue, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was also happy in that school. Not at the beginning-- I rebel. I against the rules and regulation which I was not used to it, and twice attempted to run away from school. In both time, I was captured.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 11. And once I accepted the authority, I think I became pretty good. There was a nice group of girls there. It was strictly Catholic. But we do-- did had one girl I always suspected being Jewish. But she also was, in a way, hiding her Jewishness, and we became very good friends. But my recollection was just very pleasant there, and I was every intention to finish all the eight years and graduate in that school. But the war changed my plans. I never forget the day the Germans came in, which was in 1944. And that what ended that school period. The nuns got very panicky. They had hundred and some girls in their hand, and they were afraid to have the responsibility what may happen under practically German occupation, even if it was a friendly occupation, but it was still a strong occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they called all the parents, and within two day, we were shipped home. And I never had a chance to go back and see what happened to that school because soon after the war, it became part of Czechoslovakia. Can we go back now to, to before you went to school? Do you recall any changes in 1938, any laws that came into effect that affected your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I wasn't aware of laws because my parents didn't not openly discuss it from us. But I do know some strange things. For example, our automobile-- which was the only car in the whole town-- my father transferred the title to my grandmother's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which grandmother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Catholic grandmother's name. And also some of our vine-- vineyard were transferred to some of my aunts' name. I never questioned. My father was a wise businessman, and I just assumed he knew what he's doing. And he never told us why he's doing it. Just later I was thinking on how wise he was and how ahead he was going. The only incident-- I can barely recall it, but I do remember somebody-- a local young man in his early 20s or late teens broke a window in a Jewish home and everybody was [? appalled ?] by that. But even that young man, the only accuse-- [LAUGHS] only excuse I have for him, he was no longer a lo-- a local person. As a young 15, 16 year old, he left town, and he started to work in a factory in one of the cities-- I don't where-- where he got probably a lot of propaganda and lot of co-worker who might have been anti-Semitic. So when he came home visit his family, he just acted like what he learned in the city. But everybody, including the town, condemned him for his action. But that was the only thing I can recall it. The only other incident which was very historical in my life, and it was before I went to the boarding school. Exactly when the war broke out, which was in September 1939, we received an awful-- Hungary received an awful lot of Polish refugees. Most of them were running away from the Russian side. And because we happened to be under German influence, anybody who was running away from the German side of Poland, they escaped to the Russian side. But anybody who was supposedly occupied by the Russians and was anti-communist, they had to go to the western side. And Hungary considered to be one of those. And trainload of refugees was coming in. And when my mom the first time heard it on the radio there is a train passing by in our closest railroad station, which was Mezokeresztes, he convinced my-- or she convinced my father to buy a lot of cigarettes and chocolates, basket full of it. And she went to the railroad station and waited for the train to come in and give it out to people as presents. And she spotted a very nice young boy who was two year older than my brother, a nice blond, blue-eyed kids, and she felt very sorry for him. And she contacted the Red Cross if we can take him and perhaps his whole family into our custody so they wouldn't have to go into refugee camp. But they told us it would be very difficult. But they told us also they going to station them next to our town in an other small little village which had like a Boy Scout camp. And they turned that camp into a refugee camp. So this whole trainload of people eventually wind up next door to us, and we were able to visit them daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit-- excuse me. Did you visit them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I went with my parents. And eventually my mother was able to receive permission to take this young man, who was, as I said, about 12 at the time. His name was Jan Podehovich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, don't bother. I-- but we named him [INAUDIBLE]. And [INAUDIBLE] came to live with us. And the father with his friends also spent the weekend in our house. And because we had the car, we were picking them up and big-- bring them back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I never forget an incident as long as I live. One day my parents threw a pretty big party for the Polish refugees. And they were about 30 of them in our house. And they were heavy drinking. Polish people do like vodka and-- they were-- everybody, including my father, who is not a drinker, I remember they were all drinking and talking and very much politics. And somehow the subject of the anti-Semitism came up, and it turned out this people were very anti-Semitic. They were aristocratic and intellectuals. [INAUDIBLE] father was a mayor of a city next to [INAUDIBLE]. And some of them were lawyers and artist. One was an architect, I remember. I even remember some of them by their name. And they started to talk against the Jews, and my father is a very patient and very tolerant man. And it took a long time before he decided that was it. And he said it very quietly in German, which most of them did understood. Gentleman's, I think you must know you are in a Jewish man's home. And they were all shocked. We had a tradition not to display any religious artifact. Anybody who walked into our house, they didn't know if it's Catholic or Jewish. We didn't had anything in our house to give away any influence of any religious background. So they had no idea. And I assumed they were surprised to get so much generosity from a Jewish family. And they were very upset when they find out how they heard my father's feeling, and they all apologized. And they never stopped being friendly and being very-- they give my father an awful lot of respect after that. Matter of fact, there was a newspaper, an immgr-- immigrant published newspaper, Polish newspaper, and there was an article about our family in it and all the help, financial and personal help my family gave to that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This camp was primarily, then, non-Jewish refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. All of them were non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish ref-- Jewish refugee camps nearby that you know of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not as far as I know. We had a lot of Jewish refugees coming from Romania about the same time or a little bit later, more toward '40 and '41. And I did know we have refugee camps for those Jewish people, but I haven't seen it. It was nothing close to our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time-- so after that time, what, what did your father do after that? You mentioned he was taken into the labor service system?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, he was taken into the regular army. And he had-- he wore his Hungarian uniform. And he started out with the army for about a half a year or a year. I am not quite sure. And then suddenly, they reorganized the group, and he became part of their labor unit instead of the army. They did wore the army clothes without their medals or without any attachment they put on a uniform. And they had to wear a yellow band on their arm, and that was to identify them. They are part of the labor unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that yellow band have any Jewish insignia on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only a yellow band. I don't remember. Later on, I heard and read in books about identification on it. But as far as I remember, the only arm band, they had white and yellow without anything on it. White was for the christened or half Jews, and the yellow was for the fully Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw the arm band on your father's arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father leave home at that time for any period of time? Or did he come back on weekends? What was the arrangement?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know he did came home least once or twice. No, I remember definitely-- two definitely I remember. But it could have been more because later on, I was in a boarding school, and I had no time to observe when he was home. But before I went to the boarding school, once, he came home, and I was shocked to see his new type of a uniform. And the second time he came home and there was a strict restriction of not to able to leave your town. You just came home for the vacation, but if you had part of your family in an other town, you wasn't allowed to travel. But I was in a boarding school by then in Kosice, and my father, under-cover, he did travel with my mom to Kosice. And they rented a hotel room, and he wasn't leaving his hotel room. My mother picked me up at the school, and I was able to go and visit my father in that hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this hurt me because it was time for us to have dinner, and there was no room service those days. So my mother and I, we went downstairs to eat in a restaurant, but my father didn't came. And I asked my mom, why doesn't he come? And that's when my mom told me, he's not supposed to be here. He is risking his life just to see me. And that was it. Can-- do you have any other recollections of any changes in your life at that particular time between 1938 and 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, little by little I heard-- especially in a boarding school, I heard anti-Semitic remarks. But the sooner a nun would overhear it, they would put a stop to it. But I was shocked one day. One of the girl was telling stories about how the Catholics-- oh, no-- how the Jewish people would kill Catholic children and use their blood in making matzah. And I was so shocked, and I didn't know if I should mention that to my superior or not. But finally, I did not say anything. But as soon as I went home, and I had a chance to talk to my mom, I told her about the story, which made her terribly upset. But she told me, don't pay any attention to it. People have making up funny stories on both side, so it's just one of those, and just live with it. And that's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1943, you left the boarding school, and you came back home to your village. And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was in 1944 when the Germans came in, and we had to lead that boarding school very quickly. And I came home. It was still the family intact. My father was not home. He was in that labor unit. But all my relatives were still in their own home-- very restricted, not able to leave or travel. But we had no ghetto in our little town. Everybody was supervised and made sure they stay in put. But everybody knew what's coming, especially our town because earlier, perhaps a year before, there was a Czechoslovakian Jewish couple who illegally crossed the border, escaped from Czechoslovakia. One of them, the husband or the wife brother lived in our little town, Tibolddaroc. And they were able to get there. And they were telling horrible stories about what going on in Slovakia, which started deportation much earlier than Hungary did. And they knew about Auschwitz. They knew about a lot of things which we had absolutely no idea. And I am sure the stories they were telling, we couldn't even believe it. The only time we realize this people do telling the story, do telling the truth, when the Germans came home-- came into Hungary, this couple was found dead, hanging in a attic. By that time, they knew the same thing come to Hungary which came to Slovakia, and they were not about to live it through. And after that incident, everybody in town took it seriously. And in a long run, I am so thankful for life. I did had a chance to say goodbye. I knew they will be deported, even so it was no ghetto. But I knew it's a matter of days they will have to leave. And I was able to meet everybody. I remember one time my father's sister, who was an old maid, and she came over with a big, old-fashioned family album, one of those heavy duty, heavy pages. And she made my brother and I sit down, and she said, you must remember that in case you survive, you must remember your grandparents. This is how they looked like. This is their wedding photo. This is the family, and this is so-and-so. And everybody was in that book. Some of the member of the family, I never met. There was one brother of my father who emigrated to Israel in the '30s, and I don't remember them, but they were in that photo album. And we paged through patiently and slowly, and she attached a story to almost every picture. And she brought over some relics in the family. My grandmother had a beautiful Brussels lace shawl she wore in holidays, and it was always put back into the original box. My grandfather was supposed to-- bought her as a gift from Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember she even asked my mom, why don't you keep it? Why don't we keep it in here? And my mother felt it is just as danger losing it from our house and from theirs because we will probably not going to be home very long either. But she was determined. She's not going to sit around and wait till somebody come and get us. But she didn't told that to us. It was just a secret. She was planning something, but she didn't told anybody what she's planning. --

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was home for two, three days. I played with my cousins, and we did said goodbye. I remember in the dark sitting outside and looking at the sky. And I was telling my cousins which is the Big Dipper, and how to locate the North Star, which I learned in school. They did not. And we decided every time we going to see the Big Dipper, we will think each other. [SOBBING] In other word, it was a really truly goodbye. And I never saw them again, not one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My favorite cousin was Zsuszi. She was beautiful. I always thought she's the most beautiful in all the children. She reminded me of Snow White in the Walt Disney movies. She had that jet black hair, thick, jet black hair. And her face was just like in a Walt Disney She had two younger sister, Judy and Eva, and another set of cousins, Vera, Ernie, Tibor, Sarah, Pecla. It was a big family. And they were all home because they had to-- everybody had to report into their place. And they had to wait for the transportation. So I had a chance to say goodbye to every one of them. And we didn't-- I didn't stay too long. My mother decided to get me away from home. It wasn't hiding yet, but she thought I would be safer in the city of Miskolc where nobody knew us. And she had an aunt and uncle living in that city who had never had no children. And they adored my brother and me. We were everything for them. And she knew it's-- we're doing them actually a favor if we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother was not home. He was still in school, but I was sent to my great uncle and aunt to spend some time with them. In the meantime, my brother finished school, also somewhat early, but not as early as I did. And I don't know how they came up with a solution, but he joined my father in the labor unit, which was brought back from the front. They were no longer in a war.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they created a special unit of doctors, lawyers, pharmacists, and intellectuals, also some people who distinguished themselves in the First World War, which my father did. He had a couple of medals to prove. So it was through arrangement, he joined that group. And it was a promise that group will be kept safe till the end of the war because the country need them. And with that knowledge, they decided my brother would be safe with that group inside the labor unit. Nobody would know where he is. But they decided not to put him part of the group. He was never given any identification. He was not on any kind of list. The head of this group just did that as a favor. I don't know, he may received money for it, or he did it on his goodness. I really can't say. But my brother was together with my father for a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: I stayed with this aunt and uncle just for a short while because it's a very funny thing happened in the same time at that little village. There was a group of the intellectuals of the town-- the priest, one of the teacher, the head of the town, and the head of the police organization, which was not police, but we called that the [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know what the English translation, but in a books, I heard-- I read it [INAUDIBLE]? I don't know if I say it correctly. But that was the organization we had in our town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on every weekend-- usually on Sunday night-- these four men would come together and play card. And the priest, the Catholic priest, was especially very close friend of us and admired my father for his religious tolerance and also my mom. He never called it against my mom of changing religion, and he always showed a special affection to our family. And during playing card, one of the guy who was the head of the town, he happened to mention, oh, this Schvarcz family is just too wise. They are getting away with murder. But I fix their wagon. I don't know where Otto is, but I find out where he studied. And I made all kind of an arrangement. And tomorrow morning-- early morning, 5:00 or 6:00 in the morning-- the police, the Miskolc police is heading to that house where she is staying. And they going to pick her up and put her into the Miskolc ghetto because the Miskolc ghetto is going to be picked up the next day. So before the parents find out-- before that wise mother off her vin-- find out where is her daughter, the ghetto is going to disappear. And when the priest heard that, he became kind of panicky. And he didn't know what to do, but he came up with a solution. You see, one of the problem was the commuting because our town had no bus, no train service, and nobody had cars. It was almost impossible to make any arrangement. But he knew a family friend who lived in a other town who had a car and asked him--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just stop right here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd be glad to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll go on, continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.
